# claimlib bibliography

*Generated by `claimlib/build_literature.py`. Each entry's `summary` is hash-locked (`summary_sha256`) and re-verified by `claimlib/tests/test_literature.py`; every module reference resolves here.*

19 works, cited by 11 of 58 modules.

### FIPS PUB 180-4 — Secure Hash Standard (SHS)

*Standard. NIST. NIST, 2015. [https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/detail/fips/180/4/final](https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/detail/fips/180/4/final)*

The Secure Hash Standard specifies the SHA-1 and SHA-2 family (SHA-224/256/384/512) of cryptographic hash functions, defining the message padding, block processing, and round compression that map an arbitrary message to a fixed-length digest.

Cited by: `sha256`.

### FIPS 203 — Module-Lattice-Based Key-Encapsulation Mechanism Standard (ML-KEM)

*Standard. NIST. NIST, 2024. [https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/fips/203/final](https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/fips/203/final)*

The NIST post-quantum standard for key encapsulation (ML-KEM, based on CRYSTALS-Kyber), whose security relies on the hardness of module-lattice problems believed to resist attack by quantum computers; used to establish shared secrets.

### FIPS 204 — Module-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Standard (ML-DSA)

*Standard. NIST. NIST, 2024. [https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/fips/204/final](https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/fips/204/final)*

The NIST post-quantum digital signature standard (ML-DSA, based on CRYSTALS-Dilithium), a module-lattice signature scheme intended to replace RSA/ECDSA signatures against a quantum adversary.

### FIPS 205 — Stateless Hash-Based Digital Signature Standard (SLH-DSA)

*Standard. NIST. NIST, 2024. [https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/fips/205/final](https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/fips/205/final)*

The NIST post-quantum standard for stateless hash-based signatures (SLH-DSA, based on SPHINCS+), whose security depends only on the properties of its underlying hash function, generalizing the Lamport/Merkle idea to a practical many-time signature.

Cited by: `lamport`.

### SRI CSL-98 — Constructing Digital Signatures from a One-Way Function

*Paper. Leslie Lamport. SRI International (Technical Report CSL-98), 1979. [https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/publication/constructing-digital-signatures-one-way-function/](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/publication/constructing-digital-signatures-one-way-function/)*

Introduces the Lamport one-time signature, which builds a digital signature scheme from nothing but a one-way (hash) function by revealing pre-images of committed hash values; its security rests only on hash preimage resistance, making it quantum-resistant.

Cited by: `lamport`.

### Directive (EU) 2022/2555 — Directive (EU) 2022/2555 (NIS2) on measures for a high common level of cybersecurity

*Regulation. European Parliament and Council. Official Journal of the European Union, 2022. [https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/dir/2022/2555/oj](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/dir/2022/2555/oj)*

The EU NIS2 Directive raises the baseline of cybersecurity across essential and important entities; its Article 21 enumerates minimum risk-management measures (risk analysis, incident handling, business continuity, supply chain security, cryptography, MFA, and more).

Cited by: `nis2`.

### NIST CSWP 29 — The NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0

*Framework. NIST. NIST, 2024. [https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/cswp/29/the-nist-cybersecurity-framework-csf-20/final](https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/cswp/29/the-nist-cybersecurity-framework-csf-20/final)*

Version 2.0 of the NIST Cybersecurity Framework organizes cyber risk management into six Functions -- Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover -- each decomposed into Categories and Subcategories used to assess and communicate security posture.

Cited by: `nist_csf`.

### RFC 2104 — HMAC: Keyed-Hashing for Message Authentication

*RFC. H. Krawczyk, M. Bellare, R. Canetti. IETF, 1997. [https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2104](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2104)*

Defines HMAC, a mechanism for message authentication using a cryptographic hash together with a secret key, via the nested inner/outer construction with the ipad and opad padding constants.

Cited by: `hmac_sha256`.

### RFC 4226 — HOTP: An HMAC-Based One-Time Password Algorithm

*RFC. D. M'Raihi, M. Bellare, F. Hoornaert, D. Naccache, O. Ranen. IETF, 2005. [https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4226](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4226)*

Specifies HOTP, an event-based one-time-password algorithm that HMACs a moving counter with a shared secret and applies dynamic truncation to produce a short numeric code, with reference vectors.

Cited by: `hotp`, `totp`.

### RFC 4231 — Identifiers and Test Vectors for HMAC-SHA-224/256/384/512

*RFC. M. Nystrom. IETF, 2005. [https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4231](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4231)*

Provides object identifiers and a set of official test vectors for HMAC built on the SHA-2 hash functions, used to validate interoperable implementations.

Cited by: `hmac_sha256`.

### RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 Public Key Infrastructure Certificate and CRL Profile

*RFC. D. Cooper, S. Santesson, S. Farrell, et al.. IETF, 2008. [https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5280](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5280)*

Profiles the X.509 v3 certificate and CRL formats and the certification-path validation algorithm (validity windows, name chaining, basic constraints, key usage) that underpins TLS and mutual-TLS authentication.

Cited by: `pem`.

### RFC 5869 — HMAC-based Extract-and-Expand Key Derivation Function (HKDF)

*RFC. H. Krawczyk, P. Eronen. IETF, 2010. [https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5869](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5869)*

Specifies HKDF, a two-step extract-then-expand key derivation function built on HMAC: extract concentrates input entropy into a pseudorandom key, and expand stretches it into output keying material with domain-separating context.

Cited by: `hkdf`.

### RFC 6070 — PKCS #5 PBKDF2 Test Vectors

*RFC. S. Josefsson. IETF, 2011. [https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6070](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6070)*

Provides official PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA1 test vectors for a range of iteration counts and output lengths, used to validate implementations of the RFC 8018 key derivation function.

Cited by: `pbkdf2`.

### RFC 6238 — TOTP: Time-Based One-Time Password Algorithm

*RFC. D. M'Raihi, S. Machani, M. Pei, J. Rydell. IETF, 2011. [https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6238](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6238)*

Extends HOTP to a time-based variant by replacing the counter with the number of fixed time steps since an epoch, yielding codes that rotate automatically; includes SHA-1/256/512 test vectors.

Cited by: `totp`.

### RFC 7468 — Textual Encodings of PKIX, PKCS, and CMS Structures

*RFC. S. Josefsson, S. Leonard. IETF, 2015. [https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7468](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7468)*

Standardizes the PEM textual encoding used to wrap DER-encoded certificates, keys, and CMS structures: base64 of the binary content between BEGIN/END lines with a labeled encapsulation boundary.

Cited by: `pem`.

### RFC 7469 — Public Key Pinning Extension for HTTP (HPKP)

*RFC. C. Evans, C. Palmer, R. Sleevi. IETF, 2015. [https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7469](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7469)*

Defines public key pinning, where a client remembers the SHA-256 hash of a server's Subject Public Key Info (base64-encoded) and rejects future connections whose certificate chain does not present a matching pinned key.

Cited by: `spki_pin`.

### RFC 8018 — PKCS #5: Password-Based Cryptography Specification Version 2.1

*RFC. K. Moriarty, B. Kaliski, A. Rusch. IETF, 2017. [https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8018](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8018)*

Defines PBKDF2, a password-based key derivation function that iterates a pseudorandom function (typically HMAC) over a password and salt to stretch a low-entropy password into a cryptographic key.

Cited by: `pbkdf2`.

### RFC 8391 — XMSS: eXtended Merkle Signature Scheme

*RFC. A. Huelsing, D. Butin, S. Gazdag, J. Rijneveld, A. Mohaisen. IETF, 2018. [https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8391](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8391)*

Specifies XMSS, a stateful hash-based signature scheme that combines Winternitz one-time signatures under a Merkle tree to sign many messages; being hash-based, it is a post-quantum (quantum-resistant) signature standard.

Cited by: `lamport`.

### RFC 8446 — The Transport Layer Security (TLS) Protocol Version 1.3

*RFC. E. Rescorla. IETF, 2018. [https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446)*

Specifies TLS 1.3, including the handshake, the HKDF-based key schedule, and optional client authentication via certificates (mutual TLS), where each peer proves possession of the private key for a presented certificate.

Cited by: `hkdf`, `spki_pin`.
